Looking at this code, I need to analyze the buggy function and trigger testcases to understand the issue. ## Analysis The buggy function `isAppropriateEndTagToken()` checks if the pending tag name matches the last start tag name. However, this comparison is case-sensitive, which causes issues when HTML tags with different cases are used (like `` vs ``). The first trigger testcase `handlesUnclosedTitle` expects `` (uppercase closing tag) to properly close a `<title>` (lowercase opening tag). HTML is case-insensitive, so this should work, but the current implementation uses case-sensitive comparison. The second trigger testcase `parsesUnterminatedTextarea` tests that a `<textarea>` without proper closing should break on encountering a `<p>` tag. The bug is in the case-sensitive comparison. The fix is to use case-insensitive comparison for tag names. ## Fix The `isAppropriateEndTagToken()` function should use `equalsIgnoreCase()` instead of `equals()`. ## Additional Testcases I need to create testcases that: 1. FAIL on buggy code (case-sensitive comparison) 2. PASS on fixed code (case-insensitive comparison) 3. Cover different scenarios not already tested New scenarios to test: - Mixed case closing tags for other RCDATA elements (like textarea with mixed case) - Different case variations (all uppercase, all lowercase, mixed case) ```json { "fixed_code": [ "boolean isAppropriateEndTagToken() {\n return tagPending.tagName.equalsIgnoreCase(lastStartTag.tagName);\n}", "void read(Tokeniser t, CharacterReader r) {\n if (r.matches('/')) {\n t.createTempBuffer();\n t.advanceTransition(RCDATAEndTagOpen);\n // diverge from spec: got a start tag, but there's no appropriate end tag (), so rather than\n // consuming to EOF; break out here\n } else {\n t.emit(\"<\");\n t.transition(Rcdata);\n }\n}" ], "additional_testcases": [ { "path": "org/jsoup/parser/ParserTest.java", "testcase": "@Test public void handlesTextareaWithMixedCaseClosingTag() {\n Document doc = Jsoup.parse(\"\");\n Element textarea = doc.select(\"textarea\").first();\n assertEquals(\"content\", textarea.text());\n}" }, { "path": "org/jsoup/parser/ParserTest.java", "testcase": "@Test public void handlesTitleWithAllUppercaseClosingTag() {\n Document doc = Jsoup.parse(\"My Title\");\n assertEquals(\"My Title\", doc.title());\n}" }, { "path": "org/jsoup/parser/ParserTest.java", "testcase": "@Test public void handlesTextareaWithUppercaseClosingTag() {\n Document doc = Jsoup.parse(\"

after

\");\n Element textarea = doc.select(\"textarea\").first();\n assertEquals(\"some text\", textarea.text());\n assertEquals(\"after\", doc.select(\"p\").first().text());\n}" } ] } ```

Looking at the buggy function and trigger testcase, I need to analyze the issue: **Analysis:** 1. The `insert(Token.StartTag startTag)` function handles self-closing tags 2. When a tag is self-closing, it: - Inserts an empty element - Adds it to the stack - Emits an end tag token 3. The trigger testcase shows that known HTML tags (like `
`, `

I need to analyze the buggy functions and the trigger testcase to understand the issue. ## Analysis Looking at the code, I can see three methods: 1. `processStartTag(String name)` - creates a start tag with just a name 2. `processStartTag(String name, Attributes attrs)` - creates a start tag with name and attributes 3. `processEndTag(String name)` - creates an end tag The trigger testcase is parsing an HTML file with invalid table contents and checking that certain content appears in the correct order. The bug appears to be in the two `processStartTag` methods. The first method (single parameter) calls `start.reset().name(name)` and then processes it. The second method (two parameters) calls `start.reset()` and then `start.nameAttr(name, attrs)` separately before processing. The issue is that in the first method, after calling `reset()`, the `name()` method is called on the returned object from `reset()`. However, in the second method, `reset()` is called and its return value is ignored, then `name()` is called on the original `start` object. If `reset()` returns a new object or modifies state in a way that affects subsequent calls, this inconsistency could cause bugs. The most likely issue is that `reset()` should be called and then methods should be called on the `start` object directly (not chained to reset()'s return value). Looking at the pattern, the second overload seems correct: it calls `start.reset()` (ignoring return), then calls methods on `start`. The first overload chains `.name(name)` to the result of `reset()`, which may not be the intended behavior. Similarly, `processEndTag` has the same pattern as the first `processStartTag` - it chains `.name(name)` to `reset()`. The fix should make all methods consistent: call `reset()` first (without chaining), then call methods on the tag object, then process. ## Fixed Code ```json { "fixed_code": [ "protected boolean processStartTag(String name) {\n start.reset();\n start.name(name);\n return process(start);\n }", "public boolean processStartTag(String name, Attributes attrs) {\n start.reset();\n start.nameAttr(name, attrs);\n return process(start);\n }", "protected boolean processEndTag(String name) {\n end.reset();\n end.name(name);\n return process(end);\n }" ], "additional_testcases": [ { "path": "org/jsoup/parser/HtmlParserTest.java", "testcase": "@Test\npublic void testStartTagWithNameOnly() {\n String html = \"

Test

\";\n Document doc = Jsoup.parse(html);\n assertEquals(\"Test\", doc.select(\"p\").text());\n assertEquals(\"div\", doc.body().child(0).tagName());\n}" }, { "path": "org/jsoup/parser/HtmlParserTest.java", "testcase": "@Test\npublic void testEndTagProcessing() {\n String html = \"
Content
\";\n Document doc = Jsoup.parse(html);\n Elements spans = doc.select(\"span\");\n assertEquals(1, spans.size());\n assertEquals(\"Content\", spans.first().text());\n}" }, { "path": "org/jsoup/parser/HtmlParserTest.java", "testcase": "@Test\npublic void testNestedTagsWithAttributes() {\n String html = \"
Cell
\";\n Document doc = Jsoup.parse(html);\n Element td = doc.select(\"td\").first();\n assertNotNull(td);\n assertEquals(\"test\", td.attr(\"class\"));\n assertEquals(\"Cell\", td.text());\n}" } ] } ```
